strict_unit_definition: A unique named organization with public evidence that its operating system learns or improves through a documented feedback, evaluation, memory, experimentation, decision, failure-learning, specialized-agent, workflow-adaptation, knowledge-curation, or governance loop. A mere AI deployment, tool listing, hypothetical use case, theory paper, or vendor claim without an organization-level improvement loop does not qualify.
counting_rule: Strict counts include only entries individually shown to meet strict_unit_definition. Null means unknown because no row audit supports a numeric result. Zero is allowed only for a documented sample or complete audit with no qualifying entries; a sample zero does not describe unaudited rows or assert that no qualifying entry exists after a future full audit.

largest_claim.reason: No exhaustive public-corpus universe or row-level like-for-like audit has been established. Large adjacent collections use different units, and the closest repository does not expose organization case records.

retrieval_environment.exa_search: blocked: free MCP rate limit returned HTTP 429
fallbacks: GitHub public API via authenticated gh CLI

collections.name: Evidently ML and LLM system design case-study database

collections.strict_count_method: Landing-page scope and schema audit only; the 805 rows are application/system designs, not organization-level learning-loop records, and no row-level strict audit was available in the retrieved page.
collections.uncertainty: Strict count unknown: the retrieved landing page supports scope and raw volume but not stable row-level decisions.
exclusions: ML/LLM product implementations without an organizational improvement loop
generic system-design learnings
multiple use cases from one company

audit.selection_method: Landing-page scope/schema review; no individual records were counted as audited units.

retrieval_evidence.evidence: The landing page states 800 case studies from 150+ companies; the linked 2025-12-22 database-composition analysis gives the unrounded total as 805 use cases from 185 companies.

collections.uncertainty: Strict count unknown because no stable complete or sampled row audit was captured.
exclusions: cross-industry reference architectures

predictive optimization

audit.selection_method: All landing-page titles and summaries were screened at index level, but no stable row-decision ledger was captured; conservatively classified as scope-only.

collections.name: AI Trace
collections.scope: Free nonprofit transparency collection of cited, moderator-reviewed corporate AI practices, organized by named company.
collections.raw_count_method: publisher-displayed exact homepage counter; 1,602 practices and 4,528 cited sources were separate counters at retrieval
collections.strict_count_method: Deterministic audit of the six companies in the homepage Recently Updated list at retrieval. Company pages document AI use, while none of the sampled entries established an operating organizational improvement loop under the strict definition.
collections.uncertainty: Zero applies only to the six-row deterministic sample; the remaining 272 company entries were not audited and may contain qualifying evidence.
exclusions: AI adoption or automation without an improvement loop
product features
data analysis without evidence that results feed an organizational learning cycle
unaudited companies outside the deterministic sample

audit.selection_method: All six company links in homepage Recently Updated order at the 2026-07-12 retrieval; this is a freshness sample, not a random or representative sample.

Methodology defines moderator review, a practice-level schema, at least one cited source per published practice, periodic re-checking, and public append-only edit history.

collections.name: 100 Million Healthier Lives: Case Studies from Around the Globe
collections.scope: Ten named organizations using the Community of Solutions improvement framework to measure outcomes, test changes, learn from feedback and results, adapt operations, and spread successful models.
collections.raw_count_method: Publisher-stated count in the dated report and landing page, cross-checked against all ten organization case headings in the 23-page public PDF.
collections.strict_count_method: Complete report audit. Each of the ten named organization cases documents use of the Community of Solutions framework; the report defines its mutually reinforcing measurement, testing, learning, adaptation, and scaling cycle, and each row contains organization-specific evidence of at least one such loop.
collections.uncertainty: Low within this fixed report: all ten cases were audited, though inclusion still depends on interpretation of the documented framework against the strict unit definition.
exclusions: The 24 SCALE communities listed as program participants but not presented as the report's ten audited organization cases
individual partner organizations mentioned only as context
unnamed schools and local program sites
outcomes without a documented operating feedback, testing, measurement, or learning mechanism

audit.selection_method: All ten organization case headings in report order, audited from the publisher's 2020 public PDF on 2026-07-12.

rows.label: Community Solutions
rows.reason: Replaced placement totals with real-time by-name data, used gaps and individual barriers to modify outreach, and tracked progress toward functional zero.

rows.label: Health Improvement Partnership of Maricopa County
rows.organization: Health Improvement Partnership of Maricopa County
rows.reason: Set incremental targets, assessed progress frequently, ran a 100-day test, harvested lessons, and spread the resulting food-distribution model.

rows.label: National Council on Aging
rows.reason: Piloted a well-being assessment, revised questions and time horizon from participant feedback, then used results to adapt partner services and address shortfalls.

rows.label: Proviso Partners for Health
rows.reason: Learned from peer organizations, used resident-led agenda setting and an equity dashboard, and iteratively expanded partnerships and operating programs.

rows.label: Ethnic Community-Based Organization for Immigrants and Refugees
rows.organization: Ethnic Community-Based Organization for Immigrants and Refugees
rows.reason: Adopted explicit aims, measurement plans, and small tests of change; community feedback directly changed survey design and service practices.

rows.label: LiveWell Kershaw Coalition
rows.reason: Used assessment data, community feedback, youth-designed small tests of change, and shared measurement to redirect services and define action priorities.

rows.label: Concord Hospital
rows.reason: Used a progress-assessment framework, neighborhood data, and patient feedback to revise strategy, care plans, partnerships, and interventions.

rows.label: Community Platform for Empowerment and Development
rows.organization: Community Platform for Empowerment and Development
rows.reason: Replicated and adapted a learned empowerment program locally, measured participation and downstream outcomes, and used peer idea exchange to evolve activities.

rows.label: Youth of Solutions
rows.reason: A cross-country learning network taught driver diagrams and progress tracking; participants adapted and replicated approaches in their own schools and communities.

rows.label: The Daily Mile
rows.reason: The organization commissioned outcome and equity research, learned from adoption patterns, preserved the tested core model, and deliberately adapted its scaling system.

retrieval_evidence.evidence: The page dates the report to 2020, states that it features ten organizations, and describes co-design, co-evaluation, testing, coaching, and learning across the 2014-2020 initiative.

The report states that its ten organizations use mutually reinforcing framework components to measure effectiveness, test solutions, learn, adapt, and spread success; all ten organization headings and case narratives were audited.

collections.name: MoreSteam continuous-improvement case studies
collections.scope: Public resource index labeled as case studies, mixing named-organization Lean Six Sigma and operational-excellence cases with interviews, instructional articles, training stories, and one-off projects.
collections.raw_count_method: Complete deterministic count of the 20 cards exposed on the public case-studies index at retrieval; the newest card supplies the collection's observed update date.
collections.strict_count_method: Complete card-by-card index audit, deepened on the strongest candidates' detail pages. Included only unique named organizations with explicit recurring measurement, feedback, experimentation, capability-building, or adaptation loops; one-off modeling projects, unnamed sites, training delivery, interviews, and general articles were excluded.
collections.uncertainty: Moderate: all 20 cards were decided, but detail-page depth was concentrated on plausible inclusions and borderline one-off versus recurring-loop judgments remain interpretive.
exclusions: general methods or interviews
training delivery without an organization-level improvement loop
unnamed organizations
one-off process-modeling or restructuring projects without a recurring learning cycle
country-level ecosystem commentary rather than a single operating organization

audit.selection_method: All 20 cards in public index order on 2026-07-12; detail pages were inspected for candidate inclusions and ambiguous named-organization rows.

rows.label: Human-Powered Technology at the Center of Continuous Improvement at Veritext
rows.reason: The detail page documents a continuous NPS feedback system, Gemba observation, DMAIC measurement, frontline feedback, pilots, and changes guided by resulting data.

rows.label: Greif's Success Scaling Lean Six Sigma with MoreSteam
rows.reason: Greif piloted, measured and showcased early results, then adapted cohort training into continuous on-demand flow and scaled the learned model globally.

rows.label: How Dartmouth Health Uses Process Modeling to Predict the Unpredictable
rows.reason: Its operational-excellence team repeatedly simulates real-world variation, tests scenarios, and uses results to alter staffing and care-system decisions.

rows.label: Aurorium’s TRACtion Success – From Spreadsheets to a Single Source of Truth
rows.reason: Aurorium established a centralized improvement-project system for recurring visibility, reporting, prioritization, and review rather than a single isolated automation.

rows.label: Process Modeling Produces Transformational Recipe for Success
rows.reason: A consultant's one-off optimization of an unnamed pizza maker does not satisfy the named-organization or recurring operating-loop requirements.

rows.label: Process Modeling Fuels Operational Excellence in High-Stakes Industry
rows.reason: Medtronic used repeated simulation experiments to identify production variation, test countermeasures safely, and feed findings into operational decisions.

rows.label: A Voyage of Continuous Improvement at Mercy Ships
rows.reason: The case describes an organization-level continuous-improvement program that develops internal problem-solving capability and applies learned methods across processes.

rows.label: The Impact of Process Modeling: Reducing Queue Times for COVID Testing
rows.reason: A bounded queue-modeling intervention at an unnamed hospital does not establish a recurring organization-level learning loop.

rows.label: IISE-MoreSteam Pact Enables Blended Learning Pivot
rows.reason: A training-delivery partnership and blended-learning pivot is not evidence of IISE's operating system learning through a documented loop.

rows.reason: An individual career interview, not a named-organization operating-loop case.

rows.label: A Hot Spot for Operational Excellence
rows.reason: Country-level training and innovation commentary does not define one named operating organization with a documented improvement loop.

rows.label: Software to Help Your Organization Continue Moving Forward
rows.reason: A product-oriented interview does not evidence a named customer's organizational learning loop.

rows.label: Master Black Belt Uses Data to Help YMCA
rows.reason: The card establishes a data-supported restructuring project at YMCA of Greater Cincinnati, but not a recurring feedback or learning cycle.

rows.label: Exploring the Evolution of Continuous Improvement
rows.reason: General expert commentary across companies, not a normalized organization case.

rows.label: Rapper Highlights the Fun Side of Lean Six Sigma
rows.reason: An individual profile and educational artifact, not organization evidence.

rows.label: Creating the path for a Lean IT transformation
rows.reason: General course and transformation guidance without a named audited organization.

rows.label: Blended Learning Keeps Classroom Productive
rows.reason: Remote instructional delivery examples do not establish an organization-level operating improvement loop.

rows.label: 'Fresh Eyes' Fuel Gains for Habitat for Humanity

retrieval_evidence.method: Complete public index rendering and stable-link enumeration
retrieval_evidence.evidence: The index exposed 20 case-study-labeled cards with titles, summaries, dates, authors and stable detail URLs; cards range from named continuous-improvement systems to general articles and interviews.
Veritext's case documents continuous NPS collection, Gemba observation, DMAIC, pilots, staff/client feedback, measured changes, and repeated improvement projects.
Greif's case documents pilot-first deployment, early-result review, demand-led scaling, and adaptation from cohorts to a continuous on-demand global program.

collections.name: LeSS case studies showcase
collections.scope: Public full-case-study showcase for organizations adopting Large-Scale Scrum; a separate five-item section explicitly lacking full case studies is outside the measured scope.
collections.raw_count_method: Deterministic count of the 34 cards between the public page's LeSS Case Studies heading and its separate LeSS Adoptions without a full case study heading.
collections.strict_count_method: Reproducible first-five-card sample audit. Three unique named organizations have detail-page evidence of recurring feedback, experimentation, retrospection, or learning loops; one pseudonymous organization and one card without sufficiently explicit loop evidence in this audit were excluded. This is a sample result, not a whole-directory count.
collections.uncertainty: High for the collection total: three is only the qualifying count in a five-card prefix sample, and the other 29 full cases include pseudonyms, duplicate organizations, and unaudited candidates.
exclusions: five adoption entries explicitly listed outside the full-case-study section
pseudonymous organizations
cards not deep-audited beyond the reproducible sample
adoption or transformation descriptions without explicit operating-loop evidence

audit.selection_method: First five full case-study cards in public showcase order on 2026-07-12; each stable detail page was inspected. The deterministic prefix avoids favorable-case selection.

rows.label: Agfa Healthcare
rows.reason: The detail page documents two-week team retrospectives, cross-team retrospectives feeding a curated organizational impediment list, quarterly leadership evaluation and action, and continuously revised communities-of-practice guidance.

rows.label: Alcatel Lucent
rows.reason: The detail page describes an exploratory iterative system designed to obtain early market feedback, increase delivery transparency, and help teams and management learn new operating practices.

rows.label: Antarctica Investment Bank
rows.reason: The detail page explicitly identifies Antarctica Investment Bank as a pseudonym and states that names were changed, so it fails the unique named-organization rule even though the operating-loop content is relevant.

rows.label: BASE Company
rows.reason: The audited public evidence establishes a named transformation and feature-team adoption, but this conservative sample review did not establish a sufficiently explicit recurring organization-level learning loop.

rows.label: BMW Group - Unified Sales Platform
rows.reason: The detail page documents early and continuous product and organizational feedback, sprint experiments, knowledge-gap measurement and countermeasures, automated integration feedback, demonstrations, and backlog adaptation from customer feedback.

retrieval_evidence.method: Complete public showcase section enumeration plus deterministic prefix selection
retrieval_evidence.evidence: The page exposed 34 stable full-case-study cards, followed by a separately labeled five-entry section for adoptions without a full case study; the first five full cases were selected for row audit.
Agfa's case explicitly documents recurring team and cross-team retrospectives, a curated organizational impediment list, quarterly leadership review/action, feedback, and knowledge transfer.
BMW's case explicitly documents experimentation, early and continuous feedback, knowledge measurement, continuous integration signals, customer review, and backlog adaptation.

collections.name: AHRQ Improving Care Delivery Through Lean implementation cases
collections.scope: Government-published contract report with six Lean implementation cases drawn from five health-care organizations, including two cases from the same health system.
collections.raw_count_method: Publisher-stated six cases and complete count of the six numbered case-study sections in the public report table of contents.
collections.strict_count_method: Complete six-case audit. The report's Lakeview, Central, Grand, Suntown, Heights, and Horizon labels do not publicly identify a real named organization; Horizon is additionally part of Lakeview. Therefore none satisfies the unique named-organization requirement despite strong improvement-loop evidence.
collections.uncertainty: Low under the public-identity rule: all six cases were audited, but the zero reflects unavailable real identities rather than absence of organizational learning loops.
exclusions: organization aliases or non-identifying labels
duplicate case within the same aliased health system
organizational learning evidence that cannot be attributed to a publicly named organization

collections.name: NIST Baldrige Award recipients listing
collections.scope: Public year-by-year listing of Baldrige award-recipient entries from 1988 through 2025, with sector, recipient name, and where available profile and award-application-summary links.
collections.raw_count_method: Deterministic count of the 144 recipient-name headings beneath award-year sections in the rendered public listing, excluding the page's unrelated HEADQUARTERS footer heading. Repeat winners remain separate raw year-recipient entries.
collections.strict_count_method: Withheld. The directory is a highly plausible comparator, but 144 year-recipient entries include repeat organizations and heterogeneous profile availability. No complete or reproducible row sample was added in this lane, so a numeric like-for-like organization count would be unsupported.
collections.uncertainty: High: raw year-recipient entries are exact for the rendered snapshot, but unique organizations, repeat winners, category recognition, profile availability, and strict qualification remain unresolved.
exclusions: page footer and navigation headings
unresolved duplicate award years for the same organization
recipient entries not individually audited for a qualifying operating loop
category-recognition ambiguity where present

audit.selection_method: Public scope and deterministic raw-entry enumeration only; strict row audit explicitly deferred.

retrieval_evidence.method: Complete rendered-heading enumeration and page-structure audit
retrieval_evidence.evidence: The NIST page describes profiles as recipient success stories and application summaries as condensed applications; 144 recipient headings were counted across award-year sections from 1988 through 2025 after excluding the unrelated HEADQUARTERS footer heading.
